description: EXPLAIN evidence is only useful when parameters and data distribution resemble the real query workload.
body: Use this when reviewing query performance, indexes, joins, filters, pagination, or sort behavior. Plans built on empty fixtures or unrealistic parameters can hide sequential scans, bad join order, missing composite indexes, or cardinality errors. This does not require production data access; anonymized or synthetic representative scale is acceptable. Execution impact: identify common and worst-case predicates, compare estimated versus actual row counts when supported, verify order-by and limit behavior, and check sibling queries that share indexes. Validation signals include representative row counts, plan uses expected index or join strategy, no unexpected full scan for selective predicates, and measured timing within the target budget. Risk boundary: do not overfit an index to a single hand-picked query when writes or other reads dominate workload. Use when Database Engineering work touches schema deltas, query shape, transaction ownership, constraints, locks, and data volume. Do not use this guidance to override a more specific pack, user instruction, or repository contract. Actions: use the fact to constrain the change and test plan; inspect the current files first, keep the change scoped to the owning boundary, and record what evidence changed the decision. Validation signals include migration dry-runs, rollback checks, EXPLAIN output, constraint tests, and fixture data comparisons. Risk boundary: escalate or stop when the task involves data loss, long locks, non-rollbackable writes, silent query-plan regressions, and unsafe backfills, missing owner evidence, or live external state.
